Row1.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR signaling / ErbB signaling (KEGG: ErbB signaling pathway)
Row1.Rationale: Erlotinib is an ATP‑competitive EGFR TKI approved for NSCLC with activating EGFR mutations, where mutant EGFR (e.g., exon 19 del, L858R) creates strong oncogene addiction to ErbB signaling.[3],[5] Inhibition of EGFR phosphorylation suppresses downstream proliferative and survival signaling, inducing apoptosis in erlotinib‑sensitive EGFR‑mutant cells such as PC‑9.[5]

Row2.Pathway ID/Name: PI3K–AKT signaling (KEGG: hsa04151 PI3K–Akt signaling pathway)
Row2.Rationale: EGFR‑activated PI3K–AKT promotes survival and anti‑apoptosis in NSCLC; erlotinib decreases p‑EGFR and downstream AKT activity in sensitive cells, contributing to growth inhibition and apoptosis.[5] In resistant contexts, alternative inputs (e.g., TAM‑derived exosomal circPLK1 via miR‑186/PI3K/AKT) reactivate AKT and reduce TKI efficacy, highlighting PI3K–AKT suppression as a key determinant of sensitivity.[8]

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK / MAPK signaling (KEGG: hsa04010 MAPK signaling pathway)
Row3.Rationale: EGFR activates the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK cascade to drive proliferation and survival in NSCLC.[5],[6] Effective EGFR inhibition reduces ERK phosphorylation, contributing to erlotinib‑induced growth arrest, whereas persistent MEK/ERK activation via KRAS or microenvironmental signaling can sustain proliferation despite EGFR inhibition.

Row4.Rationale: Erlotinib‑resistant EGFR‑mutant NSCLC cells (H1975, H1650) exhibit constitutive STAT3 activation; JAK2 inhibitor TG101348 downregulates p‑STAT3, Bcl‑xL, and survivin, and restores erlotinib‑induced apoptosis and tumor growth inhibition.[5] PLK1 overexpression in EGFR‑mutant NSCLC also correlates with STAT3 pathway activation and poor survival, and PLK1 inhibition reverses EGFR‑TKI–induced STAT3 activation, underscoring STAT3 upregulation as a central resistance pathway.[7]

Row5.Rationale: In erlotinib‑resistant H1975 cells, survivin expression is increased after erlotinib exposure, supporting cell survival despite EGFR blockade.[5] JAK2–STAT3 signaling promotes expression of anti‑apoptotic genes including Bcl‑xL and survivin, and combined JAK2 inhibition plus erlotinib markedly reduces these proteins and enhances apoptosis, indicating that NF‑κB/STAT3‑linked anti‑apoptotic programs mediate resistance.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: PLK1–cell cycle signaling (KEGG: Cell cycle; PLK1 network)
Row6.Rationale: PLK1 is overexpressed in lung adenocarcinoma and squamous carcinoma and is further elevated in EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, where high PLK1 correlates with advanced stage and poor prognosis.[2],[7] In EGFR‑mutant models, PLK1 expression is positively associated with ErbB signaling activation, and PLK1 inhibitors (volasertib, BI‑2536) show nanomolar antiproliferative activity even in EGFR‑TKI–resistant cells, indicating that PLK1‑driven mitotic and transcriptional programs (e.g., c‑Myc, STAT3) support resistance to erlotinib.[2],[7]

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: PLK1–STAT3 positive feedback loop (EGFR‑TKI resistance KEGG set)
Row7.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, PLK1 interacts with STAT3 and promotes its JAK1‑mediated phosphorylation, while STAT3 transcriptionally upregulates PLK1, forming a reinforcing loop linked to EGFR‑TKI resistance.[7] EGFR‑TKI treatment induces STAT3 activation; co‑inhibition of PLK1 and EGFR (or MEK/ERK) suppresses STAT3 and AKT phosphorylation, downregulates c‑Myc, and yields synergistic antitumor effects in TKI‑resistant models, demonstrating that upregulated PLK1/STAT3 signaling drives erlotinib resistance.[7]

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR–PI3K/AKT/ERK reactivation by TAM‑exosomal circPLK1 (PI3K–Akt; MAPK)
Row8.Rationale: Tumor‑associated macrophage–derived exosomes enriched in circPLK1 are taken up by EGFR‑mutant NSCLC cells and increase phosphorylation of EGFR, AKT, and ERK, reducing apoptosis and inducing resistance to the third‑generation EGFR TKI osimertinib.[8] As erlotinib and gefitinib are standard first‑generation TKIs in the same pathway, this macrophage‑driven EGFR/PI3K/ERK reactivation exemplifies a microenvironmental bypass mechanism likely to undermine erlotinib efficacy as well.[5],[8]

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: DLGAP5–PLK1 oncogenic module (Cell cycle / mitotic spindle)
Row9.Rationale: DLGAP5 is markedly upregulated in lung adenocarcinoma and promotes proliferation by upregulating PLK1; overexpression of DLGAP5 rescues proliferation after PLK1 suppression.[4] Pharmacogenomic analysis in EGFR‑mutant LUAD shows that high DLGAP5 expression associates with increased erlotinib IC50, indicating that the DLGAP5–PLK1 module enhances mitotic robustness and decreases dependence on EGFR signaling, thereby conferring relative erlotinib resistance.[4]

Row10.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR‑TKI resistance / EGFR T790M–C797S mutation network (KEGG: EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor resistance)
Row10.Rationale: Clinical and preclinical data show that secondary EGFR T790M mutation within the ATP‑binding site is the most frequent mechanism of resistance to first‑generation EGFR TKIs such as erlotinib, increasing ATP affinity and reducing inhibitor potency.[3] Subsequent C797S mutation confers resistance to third‑generation covalent TKIs; all current approved EGFR TKIs, including erlotinib, target the ATP site, so activation of this resistance pathway via sequential EGFR mutations ultimately abrogates erlotinib efficacy.[3]

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: KRAS–MAPK–STAT3 signaling in EGFR‑wild‑type / KRAS‑mutant LUAD (KEGG: MAPK; JAK‑STAT)
Row11.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma, the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK pathway is a dominant driver, and KRAS‑mutant tumors show poor response to current systemic and targeted therapies.[6] STAT3 is essential for RAS oncogenic transformation, and combined RAF and STAT3 inhibition synergistically abrogates MEK/ERK activation and tumor growth in KRAS‑mutant models, implying that KRAS–MAPK–STAT3 activation confers intrinsic resistance to upstream EGFR blockade by erlotinib in this molecular subtype.

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: Tumor microenvironment–mediated EGFR reactivation (M2 TAM–EGFR axis)
Row12.Rationale: M2‑polarized macrophage conditioned medium and exosomes increase p‑EGFR/EGFR in H1975 EGFR‑mutant NSCLC cells and significantly raise the IC50 of osimertinib, decreasing apoptosis.[8] This TME‑driven EGFR reactivation via exosomal cargo (including circPLK1) represents a generalizable bypass mechanism that sustains EGFR and downstream PI3K/ERK signaling under TKI pressure, likely contributing to incomplete and transient responses to erlotinib in vivo.[5],[8]

Row13.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR / ErbB signaling (KEGG ErbB signaling pathway; Reactome R-HSA-177929)
Row13.Rationale: Erlotinib reversibly blocks the ATP‑binding site of mutant and wild‑type EGFR, inhibiting receptor autophosphorylation and shutting down oncogenic ErbB signaling in EGFR‑addicted NSCLC. In exon 19 del and L858R tumors, this leads to high response rates and PFS ~9–12 months, confirming EGFR pathway suppression as the core determinant of TKI sensitivity.

Row14.Rationale: EGFR activates GRB2/SOS–RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK to drive proliferation. Erlotinib reduces ERK phosphorylation in EGFR‑mutant models, but MAPK signaling can be re‑engaged by KRAS/BRAF/MAP2K1 mutations or by MET, HER2, AXL, and FGFR1 bypass, restoring proliferation despite EGFR blockade and mediating intrinsic resistance in KRAS‑mutant LUAD.

Row15.Rationale: EGFR and other RTKs activate PI3K–AKT, which promotes survival and anti‑apoptosis. Erlotinib sensitivity correlates with loss of AKT phosphorylation; resistance arises when MET amplification, HGF overexpression, PIK3CA mutation, PTEN loss, or macrophage‑derived signals keep PI3K–AKT active, blunting apoptosis and sustaining growth under EGFR inhibition.

Row16.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, EGFR‑TKI–induced apoptosis requires BIM upregulation and caspase activation once survival signaling via PI3K/AKT and MAPK is withdrawn. BIM deletion polymorphisms, NF‑κB/STAT3‑driven expression of BCL‑xL and survivin, or other anti‑apoptotic adaptations reduce cell death despite EGFR inhibition, leading to incomplete responses and early relapse on erlotinib.

Row17.Rationale: HGF/c‑MET signaling activates PI3K/AKT, RAS/MAPK, STAT3 and FAK, promoting proliferation, survival, EMT and invasion. In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, MET amplification or paracrine HGF maintains PI3K/AKT and ERBB3‑dependent signaling under erlotinib, producing primary or acquired resistance; dual EGFR+MET inhibition can resensitize models, underscoring this axis as a key bypass pathway.

Row18.Pathway ID/Name: HER2/HER3 (ERBB2/ERBB3) co‑activation within the ErbB network
Row18.Rationale: HER2 and HER3 form highly active heterodimers with strong PI3K/AKT output. Under EGFR blockade, tumor cells upregulate HER3 or increase heregulin‑driven HER2/HER3 and EGFR/HER3 dimerization, restoring downstream PI3K and MAPK signaling despite erlotinib. This adaptive ErbB rewiring contributes to acquired resistance and motivates combined EGFR–HER2/HER3 targeting.

Row19.Pathway ID/Name: EMT and lineage plasticity programs (EMT‑related networks)
Row19.Rationale: Chronic EGFR‑TKI exposure can induce epithelial–mesenchymal transition and small‑cell or other lineage transformations characterized by loss of epithelial markers, reduced EGFR dependence, increased invasion, and activation of bypass RTKs (AXL, MET, FGFR1). These EMT/plasticity states are associated with decreased erlotinib sensitivity and poor outcomes, particularly when accompanied by TP53/RB1 loss.

Row20.Rationale: AXL is frequently upregulated in EGFR‑TKI–resistant NSCLC and is tightly linked to EMT. Activated AXL sustains AKT, MAPK, and NF‑κB signaling independently of EGFR, allowing cells to proliferate and survive under erlotinib. Genetic or pharmacologic AXL inhibition reduces growth and restores TKI sensitivity in resistant models, establishing AXL as a prototypic bypass RTK.

Row21.Pathway ID/Name: FGF2–FGFR1 autocrine loop (FGFR signaling; KEGG hsa04010 sub‑branch)
Row21.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant PC‑9 models chronically exposed to EGFR‑TKIs, tumor cells establish an FGF2–FGFR1 autocrine circuit that maintains ERK activation independently of EGFR. Inhibition of FGFR1 or FGF2 suppresses proliferation of resistant cells and re‑sensitizes them to EGFR‑TKIs, indicating that this MAPK‑centric loop is an important escape route from erlotinib.

Row22.Pathway ID/Name: IGF‑1R / IGF axis (KEGG JAK‑STAT and PI3K‑related signaling)
Row22.Rationale: Upregulated IGF‑1R signaling and reduced IGF‑binding proteins sustain PI3K/AKT and MEK/ERK activity when EGFR is inhibited, providing an alternative survival and proliferation input. Preclinical data show that co‑targeting IGF‑1R with EGFR‑TKIs enhances pathway suppression and apoptosis, highlighting IGF‑1R as a microenvironmental and autocrine driver of erlotinib resistance.

Row23.Pathway ID/Name: IL‑6 / JAK / STAT3 inflammatory signaling (KEGG hsa04630)
Row23.Rationale: MET and HER3‑containing complexes, as well as inflammatory cytokines like IL‑6, activate JAK2/STAT3, which drives transcription of anti‑apoptotic genes (BCL‑xL, survivin) and promotes EMT and immune evasion. EGFR‑TKI–resistant EGFR‑mutant cells show constitutive STAT3 activation; JAK2 or PLK1 inhibition downregulates STAT3 signaling and restores TKI‑induced apoptosis, implicating IL‑6/JAK/STAT3 upregulation as a central resistance pathway.

Row24.Rationale: Overexpression of ABC transporters reduces intracellular accumulation of TKIs, functionally attenuating EGFR, PI3K/AKT, and MAPK inhibition. Although less mechanistically detailed than RTK bypass, increased drug efflux can cooperate with signaling adaptations to diminish erlotinib exposure at the tumor cell level and contribute to clinical resistance.
